{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

::: {#exr-prac-linreg-lik}
#### Likelihood for simple linear regression
(adapted from @kleinbaum2014applied, Chapter 6, and @dobson4e, Chapter 6)

Suppose $Y_i \mid x_i \simind \Normal(\mu_i, \sigma^2)$,
where $\mu_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$.

**(a)** Write the likelihood $\Lik(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2; \vy, \vx)$
for observed data $(y_i, x_i)$, $i = 1, \ldots, n$.

**(b)** Write the log-likelihood $\ell(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2; \vy, \vx)$
and show it simplifies to

$$
\ell
= -\frac{n}{2}\logf{2\pi\sigma^2}
  - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\text{RSS},
$$

where $\text{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \mu_i)^2$.

**(c)** Explain why maximizing $\ell$ over $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$
is equivalent to minimizing $\text{RSS}$.
:::

::: {.solution}

**(a)**

$$
\Lik(\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2; \vy, \vx)
= \prod_{i=1}^n (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2}
  \expf{-\frac{(y_i - \mu_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}}
$$

where $\mu_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$.

**(b)**

$$
\ba
\ell
&= \sum_{i=1}^n \logf{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2}\expf{-\frac{(y_i-\mu_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}}}
\\
&= -\frac{n}{2}\logf{2\pi\sigma^2}
   - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \mu_i)^2
\\
&= -\frac{n}{2}\logf{2\pi\sigma^2}
   - \frac{\text{RSS}}{2\sigma^2}
\ea
$$

**(c)**

Since $-\frac{n}{2}\logf{2\pi\sigma^2}$ does not depend on $\beta_0$ or $\beta_1$,
and $\sigma^2 > 0$,
maximizing $\ell$ over $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$
is equivalent to maximizing $-\frac{\text{RSS}}{2\sigma^2}$,
which is equivalent to minimizing $\text{RSS}$.

This shows that for Gaussian errors,
the **maximum likelihood estimator** equals the **ordinary least squares estimator**.

:::
